mnemonics.title: Permutation vs Combination
mnemonics.content: Remember: P = Position matters, C = Combination (no order). Think: 'Order Matters? Pick Permutation!' and 'Combination is for Committees (no order)'.

mnemonics.title: Factorial Formula
mnemonics.content: n! = n × (n-1)! — 'To find factorial, multiply by factorial of the next smaller number'.

mnemonics.title: Circular Arrangement
mnemonics.content: For n around a circle: (n-1)! — Think: 'One to fix, rest to arrange' (fix one person, arrange the remaining).

mnemonics.title: Inclusion-Exclusion
mnemonics.content: Add individual sets, subtract intersections, add triple intersections back. 'Union = Sum - Sum + Sum'.

mnemonics.title: Pigeonhole Principle
mnemonics.content: If n > m, then at least one box has >1 item. Think: 'More items than boxes means crowded boxes!'

acronyms.expansion: Permutation of n objects taken r at a time
acronyms.usage: When order matters in arrangements. Formula: n!/(n-r)!

acronyms.expansion: Combination of n objects taken r at a time
acronyms.usage: When order doesn't matter in selections. Formula: n!/[r!(n-r)!]
Used in counting arrangements of n distinct objects
Alternative notation for combinations, also written as C(n,r)

visualTips.visualization: Think of filling r positions from n items: first position has n choices, second has (n-1), third (n-2)... Product = n × (n-1) × ... × (n-r+1) = n!/(n-r)!
Take all permutations P(n,r), then divide by r! because order doesn't matter in combinations. C(n,r) = P(n,r)/r! = n!/[r!(n-r)!].
Draw a circle, fix one person at top (12 o'clock position). Now arrange remaining (n-1) people in a line around the circle. Total: (n-1)! arrangements.
Represent n identical items as stars: ***. Place k-1 dividers (bars) to create k bins. Example: **|*|*** means 2 in bin1, 1 in bin2, 3 in bin3. Total placements: C(n+k-1, k-1).

analogies.analogy: Permutations are like seating arrangements in a theater (who sits where matters), combinations are like picking team members (just who is in the team, not their position).
If you have 3 shirts and 2 pants, you have 3×2 = 6 outfits. This is like a decision tree: 3 branches for shirts, each with 2 sub-branches for pants.
If you have 10 pairs of socks but only 9 drawers, at least one drawer must hold 2 pairs. With more items than containers, sharing is unavoidable.
Counting students who like math OR English: add those who like each, subtract those counted twice (who like both), because we double-counted the intersection.

analogies.analization: Factorial is like counting ways to arrange books: for 5 books, 1st book has 5 choices, 2nd has 4 (remaining), 3rd has 3... total 5×4×3×2×1 = 5! = 120 ways.
